title: Maintenance tasks that extend appliance life
subtitle: Cleaning and servicing common household appliances before they fail
excerpt: A washing machine replaced after 7 years instead of 14 doubles the embodied carbon of that function over a lifetime. Most early appliance failures are preventable with basic maintenance. This guide covers the key tasks for the five most replaced appliances.
sourceNotes: WRAP durability of household appliances research (2020). Which? appliance longevity data. UK consumer guidance from the Appliance Care Association on preventing premature failure.

glossaryTerms.definition: Keeping products in use for as long as possible to reduce the environmental cost of manufacturing replacements. Repair and maintenance are higher in the circular economy hierarchy than recycling.

The carbon emitted to manufacture and deliver a product. A washing machine generates approximately 200-300 kg CO2e to manufacture. Doubling the useful life from 7 to 14 years halves the annualised embodied carbon of laundry.

content.text: UK households replace appliances more frequently than their designed lifespan allows. A washing machine has a designed life of approximately 15 years; the average replacement age in the UK is 7 to 8 years. Each replacement incurs the
cost of manufacturing a new machine. Simple maintenance tasks prevent most of the failures that lead to early replacement.

Clean the filter monthly: the pump filter (usually behind a small panel at the front bottom) collects lint, coins, and debris that would otherwise block the pump. A blocked pump is one of the most common causes of breakdown.

Clean the door seal annually: mould in the door rubber seal causes persistent odours and can degrade the rubber. Wipe with a dilute bleach solution and leave the door ajar after each wash to allow drying.
Run a hot maintenance wash: a 90°C empty wash with a maintenance product or citric acid quarterly removes detergent residue and limescale from the drum and heater element.

Clean the condenser coils annually: the coils at the back or under the fridge dissipate heat. A layer of dust on the coils acts as insulation, forcing the compressor to work harder and run longer. Vacuum or brush the coils with the fridge unplugged.

Check door seals: close the door on a sheet of paper. If the paper pulls out easily, the seal is failing. Replacement door seals are available for most models and fit by pulling the old seal out of a groove and pressing the new one in.
Clean the filter monthly: the filter assembly at the base of the dishwasher trap collects food debris. A clogged filter recirculates food particles, reduces cleaning performance, and blocks the pump.
Descale the spray arms quarterly: limescale blocks the small holes in spray arms over time. Remove the arms (usually a quarter-turn release) and soak in white vinegar solution for an hour.
Clean the lint filter after every load: a blocked filter extends drying time, increases energy use, and is the main fire risk in domestic dryers.
Clean condenser unit (heat pump and condenser dryers) annually: remove and rinse the condenser under running water. A scaled condenser loses heat exchange efficiency and increases run time per load.

attrs.title: Right to repair
attrs.body: UK legislation since 2021 requires manufacturers of washing machines, dishwashers, fridges, televisions, and other appliances to supply spare parts for 7-10 years after the product is placed on the market. If a manufacturer refuses to supply parts for a machine within this period, they are in breach of the regulation. Parts such as door seals, filters, pump assemblies, and control boards must be available.

content.text: Maintaining appliances is the highest-return action in the
at home: it costs less than repair, prevents the embodied carbon of replacement, and takes less than an hour per appliance per year.
